data.title: Asian tech stocks bounce back after Trump tariff exemptions
data.description:
Key points:
  • Shares in Apple suppliers such as Foxconn, Goertek and Lens Tech rise
  • Trump says he will announce tariff rate on chipmakers next week with some flexibility
  • TSMC and SK Hynix shares fall on chipmaker tariff news

Shares in Asian tech companies that are major suppliers to companies like Apple rose on Monday after the U.S. government granted temporary exclusions from steep tariffs on smartphones, computers and some other electronics imported largely from China.

Shares in Foxconn , Apple's largest iPhone assembler, gained around 4% in Monday morning trade, with contract laptop maker Quanta up 7% and Inventec - which also makes artificial intelligence servers - rising 4%.

The broad index TWSE:TAIEX was up around 1%.

Shares in China's Goertek and Lens Tech - both Apple suppliers - also rose 3% and 1.1% respectively.

Shares in South Korea's Samsung Electronics , which supplies Apple and is also the second-ranked smartphone vendor in the U.S. market, gained 2%.

"We view the dizzying weekend tariff news as a step forward net positive for Apple as well as other tech names as it gives some flexibility and allows for China negotiations to hopefully take place in the coming months," Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives said in a note.

Analysts have warned that U.S. prices of iPhones could surge, given Apple's high reliance on imports from China. Apple chartered cargo flights to ferry 600 tons of iPhones, or as many as 1.5 million, to the U.S. from India, Reuters reported last week.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on social media on Sunday the electronics products excluded from his reciprocal tariffs "are just moving to a different Tariff 'bucket," and would be reviewed along with semiconductors in a national security trade investigation into the sector.

Shares of some chipmakers bucked the trend, after Trump said on Air Force One that he would announce a tariff rate on imported semiconductors over the next week, though he added there would be flexibility on some companies in the sector.

Shares in TSMC , the world's largest contract chipmaker, slipped into the red having opened up, while memory chipmaker SK Hynix fell 0.2%.

Alex Huang, vice president of Mega International Investment Services, said the market on Monday morning was reacting to Trump's exemption announcement, but the reaction may be too early as details are still to come out.

"It's impossible that semiconductors will be exempt from the tariffs, as that would eliminate the need for any bargaining chips in talks with the United States," he added.

TSMC is due to report first-quarter earnings on Thursday.

data.title: How China went from courting Trump to ‘never yield’ tariff defiance
data.description:
Key points:
  • After unsuccessfully courting Trump, Beijing takes hardline stance on trade
  • China orders foreign affairs and commerce officials to cancel vacations
  • Trump says China has panicked
  • China tried to rally international support against tariffs

China has put civilian government officials in Beijing on “wartime footing” and ordered a diplomatic charm offensive aimed at encouraging other countries to push back against U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs, according to four people familiar with the matter.

Communist Party propaganda officials have played a leading role in framing China’s response, one of the people said, with government spokespeople posting defiant clips on social media featuring former leader Mao Zedong saying “we will never yield.”

As part of the “wartime” posture, the details of which are being reported by Reuters for the first time, bureaucrats in the foreign affairs and commerce ministries have been ordered to cancel vacation plans and keep mobile phones switched on around the clock, two of the people said. Departments covering the U.S. have also been beefed up, including with officials who worked on China’s response to Trump’s first term, they said.

The combative all-of-government approach after Trump’s “Liberation Day" salvo marked a hard turn for Beijing, which had tried to avoid a spiralling trade war. For months, Chinese diplomats had tried to establish a high-level channel of communication with Trump’s administration to defend what China’s cabinet has described in state media campaigns as a “win-win” trading relationship.

Optimistic Chinese observers even held out hope for a grand bargain with Trump over trade, TikTok – and perhaps even Taiwan.

This account of how China shifted from seeking a deal to punching back with retaliatory tariffs and threatening all-out defiance is based on interviews with more than a dozen people, including U.S. and Chinese government officials, as well as other diplomats and scholars briefed on bilateral exchanges.

Four of them also described how Beijing's diplomats have been engaging other governments targeted by Trump tariffs, including sending letters seeking cooperation to several countries. Longstanding U.S. allies in Europe, Japan and South Korea have also been contacted, two people said.

Most of the people spoke on condition of anonymity to describe confidential government deliberations.

China's ministry of foreign affairs did not return a request for comment. A spokesperson for China’s embassy in Washington said in response to Reuters' questions that Beijing did not want to fight trade wars “but is not scared of them.”

“If the U.S. puts its own interests over the public good of the international community and sacrifices all countries’ legitimate interests for its own hegemony, it will for sure meet stronger opposition from the international community,” the official said.

The South Korean and Japanese embassies in Washington did not immediately respond to a request for comment on talks between their countries and China.

After the initial Chinese retaliation, Trump said: "China played it wrong, they panicked - the one thing they cannot afford to do!” He has also suggested that Beijing wanted to make a deal but “they just don't know how quite to go about it."

U.S. officials have also blamed China for the impasse because its trillion-dollar trade surplus with the world is the result of what they see as abuses of the global commerce system that haven’t been successfully addressed through years of negotiations.

Trump on April 2 stunned the world with massive tariffs that he said would prevent countries like China from “ripping off” the U.S. Chinese leader Xi Jinping ditched official caution and issued a patriotic message casting doubt on whether American voters could bear as much hardship as the Chinese.

The “Liberation Day” levies have since been suspended for all countries except China for 90 days. With some exceptions, trade of goods between China and the U.S. is now largely frozen, and Beijing is starting to crack down on trade of services, while warning its citizens against travel to the U.S. and putting curbs on import of American films.

POLITE START AND A QUICK STALL

Even after Trump was elected on the promise of high tariffs, relations with Beijing got off to a polite start. Trump invited Xi to his inauguration, which was eventually attended by Chinese Vice President Han Zheng.

Things started deteriorating soon after.

During the first Trump administration, Beijing had several high-level channels of communication, most notably between then-ambassador Cui Tiankai and Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner.

There isn’t an equivalent channel this time around, according to a Beijing official familiar with Sino-American ties, adding that China wasn’t sure who spoke for Trump on their relationship.

A Trump administration official said in response to Reuters' questions that the U.S. had "made clear to China that we want working-level contact to continue... but will not engage for the sake of engagement and in dialogues that do not advance American interests."

Chinese ambassador to the U.S. Xie Feng made unsuccessful attempts before the election to reach Trump’s billionaire ally Elon Musk, said a U.S. scholar who recently visited China for unofficial exchanges that Beijing has historically used to communicate with Washington policymakers.

Musk didn’t immediately return a request for comment.

Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi tried to meet Secretary of State Marco Rubio, a China hawk who is sanctioned by Beijing, during a February visit to New York to chair a United Nations session but did not secure a meeting. There has been no publicly disclosed exchange between the two sides’ top diplomats beyond a frosty phone call in late January.

Wang was also unsuccessful in his efforts to meet on that trip with National Security Adviser Mike Waltz, said a person familiar with the matter. Wang had held numerous talks with Waltz’s predecessor, Jake Sullivan, including an exchange that led to a rare prisoner swap.

In an interview with ABC News on Sunday, U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said there have been initial discussions through intermediaries between the U.S. and China.

"We all expect that the President of United States and President Xi of China will work this out," Lutnick said.

China's commerce ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Lutnick's remarks.

Trump told reporters this week that he would be willing to meet Xi, whom he also described as a friend. He has not detailed any specifics of a possible deal.

The Trump administration official said the U.S. had repeatedly asked Chinese diplomats if Xi would request a phone call with Trump and “the answer has consistently been ‘no.’”

International relations expert Zhao Minghao at Shanghai’s Fudan University said such outreach “totally doesn’t work in terms of the Chinese policymaking system.”

“For the Chinese side, usually there is agreement and work on the working level and then we can arrange the summit,” he said.

The way “countries which have tried to negotiate have been treated so far this year also certainly has not done much to encourage China to sit down at the table,” said Lynn Song, Chief Economist for Greater China at ING Bank.

There are some ongoing conversations between lower-level officials on both sides, according to one Chinese and three U.S. officials, though some working groups put in place by the Joe Biden administration to deal with commercial disputes, as well as treasury and military issues have been frozen.

LESSONS LEARNED

While many countries were hit by U.S. tariffs this month for the first time, China honed its response during previous bouts of the Sino-American trade war.

Drawing on lessons from Trump’s first term, China created a retaliatory playbook that includes tariffs as well as restrictions on about 60 U.S. companies and curbs on exports of rare earths.

The effort was a result of weeks of preparations by Chinese government officials who had been tasked with studying Trump’s policies and suggesting countermeasures that could be gradually scaled up, according to two people familiar with the situation.

Xi opted for a strong response, hitting back with across-the-board levies even before Trump’s announced tariffs went into effect. The duties were announced shortly before Wall Street opened on April 4 - a public holiday in China. U.S. equities dropped sharply lower.

One Chinese official briefed on the deliberations described the unusually swift response as akin to COVID pandemic-era decision making that was carried out without the customary sign offs by all relevant departments.

Some Chinese opinion leaders appeared to suggest off-ramps in the trade war.

Ren Yi, a political blogger with nearly 2 million followers on the Weibo microblogging platform said in an April 8 post that countermeasures “do not require a broad increase in tariffs on American goods.”

Ren, whose grandfather was a prominent reformist leader in the 1980s, suggested targeted moves like suspension of fentanyl cooperation and further restrictions on agricultural imports and movies.

China’s finance ministry said Friday that with tariffs on U.S. goods now at 125%, it will stop matching any future hikes in duties by Washington, whose tariff strategy it branded a “joke”.

‘NEVER YIELD’

China’s foreign ministry has summoned many of the heads of its overseas missions back to Beijing for a special meeting held this week to coordinate the response, according to two Beijing-based diplomats.

China has also sent formal letters to government officials of other countries pressured by Trump to engage in trade negotiations.

The letters, which were described to Reuters by four people familiar with their contents, outlined the Chinese position as well as the need for multipolarity and for countries to stand together. The messaging also included criticism of U.S. policy that echoed China's public statements.

China has approached some G20 governments with wording for a joint declaration voicing support for the multilateral trading system, an EU diplomat told Reuters.

But the diplomat said that the messaging did not address concerns also held by non-U.S. governments about Chinese overcapacity, its subsidy regime and alleged unfair competition.

Beijing has said those concerns are overblown and that the rise of its high-tech industries is due to its comparative advantages and benefits the world.

China is also heavily focused on the domestic reaction to the tariffs, with social media users this week widely reposting an April 7 editorial in the official People’s Daily warning against panic.

China has also recently started encouraging households to spend more and has dramatically changed its language about domestic consumption. Beijing is aiming to shift the engine of growth from exports to consumers at a time when the economy remains hobbled by a crisis of failed real estate development.

“The real battlefield is on the domestic front, rather than bilateral negotiations,” said Zhao of Fudan University.

Chinese officials also published on Musk’s X platform a clip of Chairman Mao giving a speech in 1953 - the last time the U.S. and China were in direct military conflict during the Korean War.

In the clip, Mao, whose oldest son died in the war, says peace is up to the Americans.

“No matter how long this war is going to last, we’ll never yield,” he said. “We’ll fight until we completely triumph.”

data.title: Taaleem to Launch Second Harrow International School in Dubai
data.description:
Taaleem (DFM:TAALEEM) intends to launch a second Harrow International School in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, after obtaining approval from the Knowledge and Human Development Authority.

The Dubai-listed education services company partnered with Harrow International Schools to open the school, which offers a British-style curriculum, according to a Sunday release.

The school will occupy a 50,000-square-meter site on Hessa Street, enrolling up to 1,800 students. The school is scheduled to be operational starting the academic year 2026.

data.title: Japan's Prime Minister Warns U.S. Trade Policy Could Change World Economic Order
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Japan's prime minister has warned that President Trump's trade policy could change the landscape of the world economy, calling for close cooperation among countries that are facing the same challenges.

"We are well aware that [U.S. economic policies] have the possibility, or perhaps the risk, of fundamentally changing the current global economic order," Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba told a parliamentary committee.

"At that time, we must consider what kind of response is in our country's national interest and in the interest of the entire world," he said Monday.

Ishiba added that it would be important for Japan to cooperate closely with countries that have relationships or alliances with the U.S., and are dealing with similar issues around higher U.S. tariffs. He also emphasized the need to work closely with Asean nations.

The prime minister said Japan shouldn't make any premature decisions just to reach a deal with the Trump administration.

He added that he expects Ryosei Akazawa, the economy minister who is leading the Japanese side in trade talks with the U.S., to sort out discussion points when he visits the U.S. later this week.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said in a post on X that he is willing to discuss with Japan about tariffs, non-tariff trade barriers, currency issues, and government subsidies.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: US 10-Year Yield Holds Near 4.5%
data.description:
The yield on the US 10-year Treasury note hovered just below 4.5% on Monday, holding steady after last week's sharp rise driven by mounting concerns over the potential economic fallout from President Donald Trump’s escalating trade war.

The recent selloff in Treasuries signaled growing investor unease and a potential erosion of confidence in US government debt as a traditional safe-haven asset.

Meanwhile, Trump exempted electronics from his new reciprocal tariffs, although he clarified that they were still subject to the existing 20% Fentanyl Tariffs.

US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick also said that these imports would face separate new levies within the next two months.

On the macro front, data from the University of Michigan showed consumer sentiment plunged in April to its lowest level since 2022, while one-year inflation expectations surged to the highest reading since 1981, reinforcing fears of persistent inflationary pressures.

data.title: Bossard Group Elects New Chair
data.description:
Bossard Group appointed David Dean as its chairman at the company's annual general meeting.

Dean succeeded Thomas Schmuckli, who stepped down, according to an April 11 release by the Swiss industrial fastening and assembly technology group.

data.title: China Railway Signal & Communication, Subsidiary Recognized in Two New Innovation Platforms
data.description:
China Railway Signal & Communication and its subsidiary, CASCO Signal, were recognized in two new innovation platforms, according to a Hong Kong Stock Exchange disclosure on April 11.

The Chinese train control systems manufacturer was recognized as the Beijing Key Laboratory for Intelligent Low-Altitude Airspace Management, and CASCO Signal was recognized as the Sixth-Batch Industrial Technology Foundation Public Service Platform under the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology.

The recognition is expected to boost the company's brand influence and development, the disclosure said.

The company's Hong Kong shares rose over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Gold prices ease from record highs as Trump grants tariff exemptions
data.description:
Key points:
  • Gold hit record high of $3,245.42 in early trade
  • Goldman Sachs raised end-2025 gold price forecast to $3,700/oz
  • Prices could eye off a run towards $3,300 in near term - analyst

Gold prices retreated on Monday from a record high hit earlier in the session as trade tensions eased after U.S. President Donald Trump exempted smartphones and computers from "reciprocal" U.S. tariffs.

Spot gold was down 0.1% at $3,232.45 an ounce, as of 0329 GMT. Bullion hit a record high of $3,245.42 earlier in the day.

U.S. gold futures edged 0.1% higher to $3,248.20.

"Softer U.S. dollar has been assisting gold, but news of tech product tariff exemptions lifted risk appetite and caused safe-haven demand to ease. This has caused gold to lack clear direction," said KCM Trade chief market analyst Tim Waterer.

The White House announced the exclusions from steep reciprocal tariffs on Friday. However, Trump bore down on Sunday on his administration's latest message that the exclusion of smartphones and computers from his reciprocal tariffs on China will be short-lived.

"Ongoing trade and tariff dramas have created higher volatility and uncertainty levels in financial markets, and in such an environment the gold price could be eyeing off a run towards $3,300 in the near term should dollar weakness persist," Waterer said.

Non-yielding gold is traditionally viewed as a hedge against economic uncertainty and inflation.

Gold prices vaulted on Friday over the $3,200-per-ounce mark for the first time as intensifying U.S.-China trade tensions rattled global markets.

Goldman Sachs raised its end-2025 gold price forecast to $3,700 per ounce from $3,300, citing stronger-than-expected central bank demand and boosted ETF inflows.

Traders see around 80 basis points worth of cuts by the end of 2025. (FEDWATCH)

Bullion tends to thrive in a low-interest-rate environment.

Elsewhere, price premium for gold in top consumer China widened last week due to consumers and investors seeking refuge from the country's escalating trade war with the United States, analysts said.

Spot silver lost over 1% to $31.91 an ounce, while platinum added 0.6% to $948.45 and palladium gained 0.8% to $922.98.

data.title: British Columbia Investment Places BBGI Global Infrastructure Takeover Timetable on Hold
data.description:
British Columbia Investment Management Corp. confirmed on April 11 that the timetable for its takeover of infrastructure investor BBGI Global Infrastructure was temporarily suspended to allow time for the satisfaction of other regulatory conditions.

British Columbia Investment is making the offer through the newly formed limited partnership Boswell Holdings 3. The proposal is now expected to be declared unconditional in the second quarter, subject to the receipt of foreign direct investment clearance in the UK.

At the time of suspension, the proposal had already received clearance from the Commonwealth of Australia. Shareholders of the target company may continue to accept the offer.

data.title: Australian gold stocks retreat from record highs as bullion dips
data.description:
** Australian gold stocks trade up 1.4% at 12,177.30 points

** Stocks pare early gains after rising as much as 2% to a record high of 12,254.40 points

** Sub-index on track for a third session of gains, if current trend holds

** Gold prices drop as U.S. President Donald Trump eased trade tensions by granting tariff exemptions on smartphones, computers and other electronics primarily imported from China

** Analysts at UBS see gold miners looking well-placed, heading further into 2025, assuming a "stronger for longer" gold price environment

** Shares of gold miners Evolution Mining and Northern Star Resources up 2% and 0.2%, respectively

** Sub-index up 42.5% this year, as of last close

data.title: Shareholders that lost money on SoundHound AI, Inc.(SOUN) Urged to Join Class Action - Contact Levi & Korsinsky to Learn More
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 13, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your SoundHound AI, Inc. investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/soundhound-ai-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=142803&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against SoundHound AI, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between May 10, 2024 and March 3, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (i) the material weaknesses in SoundHound's internal controls over financial reporting impaired the Company's ability to effectively account for corporate acquisitions; (ii) in addition, the Company overstated the extent to which it had remediated, and/or its ability to remediate, the material weaknesses in its internal controls over financial reporting; (iii) as a result of the foregoing material weaknesses, SoundHound's reported goodwill following the Amelia Acquisition was inflated and would need to be corrected; (iv) further, SoundHound would likely require extra time and expense to effectively account for the SYNQ3 and Amelia Acquisitions; (v) the foregoing increased the risk that the Company would be unable to timely file certain financial reports with the SEC; and (vi) as a result, the Company's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in SoundHound AI, Inc. stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/soundhound-ai-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=142803&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

data.title: Philippine Seven Corp Confirms Capex Of Between 5-6 Billion Pesos For 2025
Shanghai Lingang 2024 Profit Rises 3%; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Shanghai Lingang posted 2024 net profit attributable to shareholders of 1.10 million yuan, up 3.1% from 1.06 billion yuan the previous year.

Earnings per share ticked up to 0.43 yuan from 0.42 yuan, according to a filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The energy company's revenue increased 57% year over year to 11.1 billion yuan from 7.06 billion yuan.

Shanghai Lingang declared a dividend of 0.20 yuan per share.

Shares of the company were up 4% in recent trade.

data.title: Philippines says voiced South China Sea concerns during ASEAN-China negotiations
data.description:
The Philippines voiced concerns on the South China Sea, including incidents that endangered its vessels and personnel, during negotiations between ASEAN and China for a code of conduct in those waters, its foreign ministry said on Monday.

The Philippines, which hosted the latest round of talks last week, also reiterated its commitment to resolving disputes peacefully and pursuing constructive diplomatic approaches in managing differences at sea, the ministry said in a statement.

The South China Sea remains a source of tension between China and its Southeast Asian neighbours, with ties between Beijing and U.S. ally Manila at their worst in years amid frequent confrontations that have sparked concerns they could spiral into conflict.

In February, the Philippines coast guard accused the Chinese navy of performing dangerous flight manoeuvres near a government aircraft patrolling a disputed shoal in the South China Sea, an account Beijing disputed.

The Association of Southeast Asian Nations and China pledged in 2002 to create a code of conduct, but it took 15 years to start discussions, and progress has been slow.

During the negotiations from April 9-11, the countries also tackled so-called "milestone issues," the Philippines' foreign ministry said. These issues refer to critical points, including the code's scope and whether it can be legally binding.

Beijing claims sovereignty over most of the South China Sea, which it asserts through a fleet of coast guard and fishing militia that some neighbours accuse of aggression and of disrupting fishing and energy activities in their exclusive economic zones.

China insists it operates lawfully in its territory and does not recognise a 2016 arbitration ruling that said its claim has no basis under international law.

"The meeting was an opportunity for the Philippines to strongly call for the need to adhere to international law, particularly the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, and the 2016 South China Sea Arbitral Award," the ministry said.

data.title: Indian fugitive accused in nearly $2 bln bank fraud arrested in Belgium, source says
data.description:
Indian fugitive Mehul Choksi, the jeweller accused of being involved in an alleged fraud of nearly $2 billion against Punjab National Bank in 2018, has been arrested in Belgium, a source with India's Enforcement Directorate told Reuters.

The Indian government had sent a request for Choksi's extradition prior to his arrest, but he is likely to challenge it on medical grounds, the source added.

Punjab National Bank (PNB), India's second largest state-run lender by assets, had announced in 2018 that it had discovered alleged fraud worth $1.8 billion at a single branch in Mumbai.

The bank had filed a criminal complaint with India's federal investigative agency against several entities including billionaire jeweller Nirav Modi and Choksi, his uncle and the managing director of Gitanjali Gems , saying they had defrauded PNB.

Last week, a Pakistani-born Canadian businessman accused of helping orchestrate the 2008 attacks in Mumbai landed in New Delhi after the U.S. extradited him in the first such transfer in a terrorism case.

data.title: Olympio Metals Completes Diamond Drilling at Canada Project
data.description:
Olympio Metals completed a 10-hole diamond drilling program at the Chevrier and Dasserat prospects in the Dufay project in Canada, according to an April 10 filing with the Australian bourse.

Drill core from the project is being logged and sampled, with assay results expected in the June quarter, the filing said.

The company is reviewing historical data for the recently acquired Bousquet project, with drilling expected to begin in July, per the filing.

data.title: LondonMetric Property Confirms Indicative Takeover Offer for Urban Logistics REIT
data.description:
LondonMetric Property confirmed on April 11 that it submitted an indicative takeover proposal for fellow British real estate investment trust Urban Logistics REIT .

LondonMetric has until May 9 to decide whether to formalize the offer. It cautioned that a transaction cannot be guaranteed.

If pursued, the formal offer will not be made under less favorable terms than LondonMetric's acquisition of Urban Logistics shares over the last three months, during which it acquired 5,600,382 shares at up to 1.325 pounds sterling apiece.

data.title: Australia's MA Financial gains on higher AUM
data.description:
** MA Financial rises 2% to A$6.415, highest since April 4

** Diversified financial services provider records Q1 FY25 assets under management (AUM) of A$10.3 bln ($6.48 bln), up 7% y/y

** Stock set for third consecutive session of gains

** YTD, MAF up 4.8%

($1 = A$1.5906)

data.title: Oil Prices Likely Still Face Downside RisksMarket Talk
data.description: Oil prices continue to face downside risks, three Goldman Sachs Commodities Research members say in a report. Brent crude oil's price is expected to weaken and average $63 a barrel for the rest of 2025 and $58 a barrel in 2026, the analysts say. The WTI crude oil price is expected to fall and average $59/bbl for the rest of this year and $55/bbl next year, they say. Global oil demand will likely be soft given the unfolding trade war, while compliance with OPEC8+ production will probably pick up somewhat in the coming weeks partly due to signals from the group's leadership, they add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are last at $61.32/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are last at $64.57/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: China's Exports Surge as Orders Frontloaded Before Tariffs
data.description:
China's export growth in March was stronger than expected, with shipments likely front-loaded before expected U.S. tariffs.

Outbound shipments in March rose 12.4% compared with the same period a year earlier, according to the official data released Monday. That was up sharply from a 2.3% increase in the first two months and the 4.4% growth tipped by a Wall Street Journal survey of economists.

The higher-than-expected export growth came before hefty tariffs announced by U.S. President Trump came into effect in April. These tariffs are set to weigh on China's exports this month or months ahead.

China's imports fell 4.3% on year in March, better than the 8.4% decline in the January-February period, but weaker than the 1.8% rise expected by the survey of economists.

That put China's trade surplus at $102.64 billion in March, bigger than $77 billion projected by the survey.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: First Avenue for Real Estate Development Creates Business, Projects Update Report
data.description:
First Avenue for Real Estate Development (SASE:9610) established a new business and projects update report to improve transparency and disclosure framework.

The report will be updated quarterly to keep investors and shareholders informed about the real estate company's business developments and financial performance, according to a Sunday release.

data.title: Formosa Chemicals and Fibre Incurs NT$443 Million Loss in Q1
data.description:
Formosa Chemicals and Fibre incurred a consolidated after-tax loss attributable to owners of NT$442.7 million in the first quarter, or a loss per share of NT$0.08, according to a Friday filing with the Taiwan Stock Exchange.

The company attributed the loss to a slowdown in market demand, price competition stemming from industry overcapacity, and weak post-holiday order recovery.

Operating revenue shrank 6% annually to NT$78.5 billion, due to lower sales volume and falling prices, the company said.

data.title: Tasly Pharmaceutical Gets Nod to Trial Drug for Recurrent Glioblastoma
data.description:
China's National Medical Products Administration approved Tasly Pharmaceutical's application to conduct clinical trials on a drug for recurrent glioblastoma, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Saturday.

The P134 cell injection, which was developed by the pharmaceutical company and the Beijing Neurosurgical Institute, will undergo phase 1 trials for patients with the condition.

The pharmaceutical company invested 49.6 million yuan into the research and development of the drug, the filing said.

data.title: Stelar Finds Tin Anomalies at New South Wales Project
data.description:
Stelar Metals discovered tin anomalies at eight prospects at its Trident Project in New South Wales, following a review of historical data, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company said it will recommence lithium exploration at the project once market conditions improve, the filing said.

data.title: OpenLearning Teams Up with Education Center of Australia to Acquire Digital Learning Provider
data.description:
OpenLearning partnered with Education Center of Australia (ECA Group) to acquire the Employability Advantage business, a provider of digital work-readiness content and experiential learning programs, according to an April 11 filing with the Australian bourse.

The Australian Securities Exchange granted the company a waiver to allow the acquisition from ECA Investments Group without shareholder approval, subject to certain disclosures, for a total consideration of AU$1, the filing said.

ECA Group holds 100% of the capital in ECA Investments Group, which is controlled by a substantial shareholder in the company, per the filing.

data.title: KALENDER-Termine Politik/Vermischtes bis 20. April
data.description:
*************************************************************

Weitere ausführliche Terminübersichten erhalten sie unter:

* FIRMEN/MÄRKTE

* KONJUNKTUR/GELDPOLITIK

*************************************************************

Alle Zeitangaben in Central European Time (CET)

SATURDAY APRIL 12, 2025

Direct nuclear talks to take place between US, Iran - President Donald Trump made a surprise announcement on Monday (April 7) that the United States and Iran were poised to begin direct talks on Tehran's nuclear program, but Iran's foreign minister said the discussions in Oman would be indirect.

Turkey hosts Antalya Diplomacy Forum, Syria's Sharaa and Russia's Lavrov to attend - Turkey hosts the Antalya Diplomacy Forum in the Mediterranean city of Antalya on April 11-13, with various foreign leaders attending including Syria's President Ahmed al-Sharaa and Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov. Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan is expected to deliver a speech.

Gabon to hold presidential election - Gabon is expected to hold a presidential election in a step towards establishing democratic rule in the Central African country.

SUNDAY APRIL 13, 2025

Antalya Diplomacy Forum continues

Ecuadoreans go to the polls to select a president in a second round of voting.

MONDAY APRIL 14, 2025

China's President Xi to meet Vietnam's PM in Hanoi as trade conflict with US widens - China's President Xi Jinping is expected to meet Vietnam's Prime Minister Pham Minh Chinh in Hanoi as the Chinese leader embarks on a three-nation Southeast Asia tour amid a widening trade conflict with the U.S.

Many government workers face Monday deadline to respond to new Trump buyout offer - Federal workers at a number of government agencies face a Monday deadline to respond to a new buyout offer from the Trump administration.

El Salvador's Bukele meets with Trump - U.S. President Donald Trump welcomes El Salvador President Nayib Bukele to the White House. They are expected to discuss a prison in the Central American nation that has received alleged foreign gang members deported from the United States.

TUESDAY APRIL 15, 2025

Nationwide protests in the US against cuts to Social Security - "Hands Off Social Security!," protests against cuts to Social Security take place across the United States.

China's President Xi Jinping undertakes a state visit to Malaysia

16:00 - Former Peruvian president Humala and wife face sentencing for money laundering - Peruvian former President Ollanta Humala and his wife, Nadine Heredia, accused of money laundering, set to hear sentencing. The charges stem from alleged illegal contributions to their 2006 and 2011 election campaigns, including those from Brazilian company Odebrecht. The Public Prosecutor's Office has requested a 20-year prison sentence for the former president and 26 years and six months for his wife.

WEDNESDAY APRIL 16, 2025

China's President Xi Jinping undertakes a state visit to Malaysia

02:00 - Canadian party leaders debate for election - The leaders of Canada's main political parties gather for a televised French language debate in Montreal

THURSDAY APRIL 17, 2025

Italy's Meloni meets with Trump - Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni meets with U.S. President Donald Trump at the White House.

China's President Xi expected to meet Cambodian PM in Phnom Penh - China's President Xi Jinping pays a state visit to Cambodia where he is expected to meet Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Manet, according to the Cambodian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Xi's three-nation Southeast Asia tour comes as he looks to consolidate ties with some of China's closest neighbours as trade tensions with the United States escalate.

01:00 - Canadian party leaders debate for election - The leaders of Canada's main political parties gather for a televised English language debate in Montreal

FRIDAY APRIL 18, 2025

Orthodox Christians mark Good Friday with Via Dolorosa procession in Jerusalem.

SATURDAY APRIL 19, 2025

Nationwide anti-Trump protests - Thousands of demonstrators in Washington D.C. and around the United States are expected to protest U.S. President Donald Trump and his policies.

SUNDAY APRIL 20, 2025

EASTER SUNDAY - NO EVENTS SCHEDULED

Investors will closely watch for any changes to the company's 2025 profit and revenue forecast, as well as details on its planned $55 billion investment in US expansion. Focus will also be impact to its medical device business from the US-China tariff war.
